report_metadata.cancer_type: Skull Base Chordoma (Clivus, Brainstem Extension, Basilar Artery Encasement)
report_metadata.stage: Locally Advanced — Clivus origin with brainstem extension and basilar artery encasement

treatments.name: Maximal Safe Endoscopic Endonasal Resection + Adjuvant Proton Beam Therapy (>70 GyRBE)

evidence_level.rationale: Gold standard multimodal approach supported by decades of institutional series from MGH, PSI, and Loma Linda; dose-escalated proton therapy data from 112-patient series demonstrates robust outcomes. Proton therapy is the established standard for skull base chordoma.

survival_benefit.rationale: 5-year OS 78%, disease-specific survival 83%, 5-year local control 74% with subtotal resection. For GTR patients, 5-year LC reaches 95% and DSS 100%. Median dose 73.8 GyRBE delivers meaningful disease control in this otherwise lethal tumor.

accessibility.rationale: Proton therapy available at ~40 centers in the US and growing internationally. Endoscopic endonasal surgery for clivus chordoma requires specialized skull base neurosurgery centers. Both modalities are standard-of-care and insurance-covered.

safety_profile.rationale: No acute grade >=3 radiation toxicity reported. Late effects include hypopituitarism (17%), temporal lobe necrosis grade 3 (5%), vision loss (<3%). Surgical risks include CSF leak (11.8%), meningitis (23.5%), cranial nerve palsy (11.8%). Basilar encasement increases surgical risk.

biomarker_match.rationale: Universal applicability to all chordomas regardless of molecular profile. Brachyury/TBXT positivity confirms chordoma diagnosis. No biomarker restriction for surgery + proton therapy.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Maximal safe surgical debulking via endoscopic endonasal transsphenoidal approach provides direct midline access to clivus tumors, reducing tumor burden while preserving neurovascular structures. Adjuvant proton beam therapy at doses >70 GyRBE exploits the Bragg peak for precise dose deposition to residual tumor with steep dose falloff protecting brainstem, optic pathways, and temporal lobes. The high linear energy transfer at the Bragg peak end yields enhanced relative biological effectiveness against radiation-resistant chordoma cells.

key_evidence.study_name: Clinical Outcomes Following Dose-Escalated Proton Therapy for Skull-Base Chordoma

treatments.availability: Standard of Care — available at major proton centers and skull base surgery centers worldwide

treatments.name: Carbon Ion Radiotherapy (CIRT) Post-Surgical Resection

evidence_level.rationale: Large institutional series from NIRS Japan (47+ patients) and GSI/HIT Germany (147 patients) with long-term follow-up. Phase III HIT-1 trial comparing proton vs carbon ion. 5-10 year follow-up data available.

survival_benefit.rationale: NIRS data: 5-year OS 87%, 5-year local control 88%, 10-year LC 80%. GSI data: 5-year OS 86%, 5-year LC 70%. Higher RBE (2.5-3.5) compared to protons theoretically superior for radiation-resistant chordoma histology.

accessibility.rationale: Carbon ion therapy available only at ~15 centers worldwide (Japan: NIRS/QST, Germany: HIT Heidelberg, Italy: CNAO, China: several). Not available in the US. Requires international travel and significant cost, though covered by national health systems in Japan and Germany.

safety_profile.rationale: Severe late toxicity <5% of patients. Approximately 12% experience some cranial nerve injury, 2% decreased visual field at >5 years. No treatment-related deaths reported in major series.

biomarker_match.rationale: Universal applicability to all chordomas. Higher RBE may be particularly advantageous for slow-growing, radiation-resistant tumors like brachyury-positive chordoma.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Carbon ions deliver higher linear energy transfer (LET) than protons, resulting in a relative biological effectiveness (RBE) of 2.5-3.5 compared to ~1.1 for protons. This produces more complex, irreparable DNA double-strand breaks in tumor cells. The sharper Bragg peak and reduced lateral scatter provide superior dose conformality for skull base targets near critical structures. The higher RBE is particularly valuable for slow-growing, radiation-resistant tumors like chordoma that are relatively resistant to conventional photon radiation.

key_evidence.study_name: Carbon Ion Radiotherapy for Skull Base Chordoma — NIRS/QST Series
key_evidence.journal: International Journal of Radiation Oncology Biology Physics

treatments.availability: Available at specialized centers in Japan, Germany, Italy, Austria, China — not currently in the US

treatments.name: Afatinib (Irreversible EGFR/HER2 Inhibitor)

evidence_level.rationale: European Phase II multicenter trial (EUROCORD) with 30 first-line patients showed 13% partial response rate and 40% progression-free at 12 months, exceeding predefined success criteria. Uniquely targets both EGFR and brachyury degradation in preclinical models.

survival_benefit.rationale: 40% PFS at 12 months in first-line setting. Median PFS for responders approximately 28 months. Disease stabilization is meaningful in a tumor with limited systemic options. Second-line cohort: 38.5% PFS at 9 months.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved for NSCLC (off-label for chordoma). Oral administration. Widely available globally. Cost manageable with insurance coverage for off-label oncology use.

safety_profile.rationale: Well-characterized toxicity profile from NSCLC use. Main toxicities are diarrhea, rash, paronychia, stomatitis — manageable with dose adjustment. Generally well tolerated in chordoma patients.

biomarker_match.rationale: EGFR is expressed in majority of chordomas. Afatinib uniquely promotes degradation of both EGFR and brachyury protein, making it particularly relevant for brachyury/TBXT-positive chordoma. No formal EGFR testing required.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Afatinib is an irreversible pan-ErbB family tyrosine kinase inhibitor that covalently binds to EGFR (ErbB1), HER2 (ErbB2), and ErbB4. In chordoma, it uniquely promotes proteasomal degradation of both EGFR and brachyury (TBXT), the pathognomonic transcription factor driving chordoma growth. This dual mechanism — inhibiting EGFR signaling and depleting the key oncogenic driver brachyury — distinguishes afatinib from other EGFR inhibitors and provides a rational targeted approach for TBXT-positive chordoma.

key_evidence.study_name: Phase 2, Single Arm, European Multi-center Trial Evaluating Afatinib in Advanced Chordoma (EUROCORD)

biomarker_requirements: EGFR expression (present in majority of chordomas)
treatments.availability: FDA-approved for NSCLC — off-label use for chordoma; oral drug, widely available

treatments.name: Imatinib (PDGFR Inhibitor)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase II study of 56 patients with advanced PDGFR-positive chordoma, the largest prospective chordoma systemic therapy trial. Additional retrospective series confirm disease stabilization. Well-established mechanism targeting PDGFRB signaling.

survival_benefit.rationale: ORR only 2% by RECIST (1 partial response in 50 evaluable patients). However, 70% stable disease rate and 64% clinical benefit rate (SD >= 6 months). Median PFS 9 months. Median OS ~30 months in retrospective data. Disease stabilization rather than regression.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved for CML/GIST since 2001. Oral medication, widely available globally, generic formulations available reducing cost. Insurance-covered. Well-characterized long-term safety.

safety_profile.rationale: No unexpected toxicities in chordoma trial at 800 mg/day. Well-characterized safety profile from decades of CML/GIST use. Common side effects include edema, nausea, fatigue — generally manageable.

biomarker_match.rationale: Requires PDGFB and/or PDGFRB expression for eligibility. Present in majority but not all chordomas. PDGFR pathway activation is common in chordoma but not universal.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Imatinib is a selective tyrosine kinase inhibitor that targets PDGFR-alpha/beta, c-KIT, and BCR-ABL. In chordoma, PDGFR-beta is frequently overexpressed and activated through autocrine/paracrine signaling. Imatinib blocks PDGFR-mediated proliferation and survival signaling, leading predominantly to cytostatic effects (disease stabilization) rather than tumor regression, consistent with targeting a proliferative pathway rather than a survival-critical oncogene.

key_evidence.study_name: Phase II Study of Imatinib in Advanced Chordoma

treatments.availability: FDA-approved (off-label for chordoma) — oral, generic available, widely accessible

treatments.name: Imatinib + Everolimus (PDGFR + mTOR Dual Inhibition)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase II study of 43 patients with combination imatinib 400mg + everolimus 2.5mg. Largest combination targeted therapy trial in chordoma. Demonstrated improved response over imatinib monotherapy.

survival_benefit.rationale: 20.9% objective response rate by Choi criteria vs 2% ORR with imatinib alone by RECIST. 76.7% clinical benefit rate. Disease stabilization in 55.8%. Meaningful improvement over imatinib monotherapy for imatinib-resistant disease.

accessibility.rationale: Both drugs FDA-approved (off-label combination for chordoma). Oral administration. Available globally. Requires oncology oversight for combination toxicity management.

safety_profile.rationale: Combination increases toxicity burden vs monotherapy. mTOR inhibitor adds mucositis, pneumonitis risk, metabolic effects (hyperglycemia, hyperlipidemia). Manageable with dose adjustments but requires monitoring.

biomarker_match.rationale: Requires PDGFRB expression. mTOR pathway activation common in chordoma, especially in imatinib-resistant tumors. Dual pathway targeting addresses resistance mechanism.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Combines PDGFR inhibition (imatinib) with mTOR pathway blockade (everolimus) to simultaneously suppress two key proliferative signaling cascades in chordoma. The PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway is often activated as a resistance mechanism to PDGFR inhibition alone, providing a rational basis for dual targeting. Everolimus inhibits mTORC1, blocking downstream proliferation and survival signals that bypass PDGFR blockade.

key_evidence.study_name: Imatinib and Everolimus in Patients with Progressing Advanced Chordoma: A Phase 2 Clinical Study

treatments.availability: Both drugs FDA-approved — off-label combination use for chordoma

treatments.name: Lapatinib (EGFR/HER2 Dual Kinase Inhibitor)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase II study of 18 patients with EGFR-positive chordoma. Published data with RECIST and Choi response criteria. One of the few completed phase II trials in chordoma with published results.

survival_benefit.rationale: 33% partial response rate by Choi criteria (6/18 patients), 39% stable disease (7/18). Median PFS 8 months by RECIST. Modest but meaningful antitumor activity. Inferior to afatinib preclinically.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved for HER2+ breast cancer. Oral medication, widely available. Off-label use for chordoma possible with oncologist prescription.

safety_profile.rationale: Well-characterized toxicity from breast cancer experience. Main side effects diarrhea, rash, fatigue. Generally manageable.

biomarker_match.rationale: Requires EGFR expression — present in majority of chordomas. Also targets HER2. Does not degrade brachyury unlike afatinib.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Lapatinib is a reversible dual tyrosine kinase inhibitor targeting EGFR (ErbB1) and HER2 (ErbB2). In chordoma, EGFR is frequently overexpressed and drives proliferative signaling. Lapatinib blocks ATP binding to the intracellular kinase domain of both receptors, inhibiting downstream MAPK and PI3K/AKT signaling cascades that promote tumor cell proliferation and survival.

key_evidence.study_name: Phase II Study on Lapatinib in Advanced EGFR-Positive Chordoma

treatments.availability: FDA-approved for HER2+ breast cancer — off-label for chordoma

treatments.name: Stereotactic Radiosurgery (SRS) for Recurrent/Residual Disease

evidence_level.rationale: Multiple institutional case series for recurrent chordoma. Used as salvage after prior particle therapy or as boost. CyberKnife and Gamma Knife series published. Not first-line but established salvage modality.

survival_benefit.rationale: Salvage local control variable — depends on prior treatment and tumor volume. Useful for focal recurrence or residual tumor. 9% achieve sustained control after recurrence following proton therapy. Benefit mainly in focal disease.

accessibility.rationale: SRS widely available at most major medical centers. CyberKnife and Gamma Knife platforms well-established. Insurance-covered for salvage treatment. Does not require specialized particle therapy infrastructure.

safety_profile.rationale: Single-fraction or hypofractionated delivery limits cumulative normal tissue dose. Risk depends on prior radiation exposure. Brainstem proximity limits maximum dose in skull base location. Well-tolerated in appropriately selected patients.

biomarker_match.rationale: No biomarker requirement. Applicable to all chordoma subtypes and molecular profiles.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Stereotactic radiosurgery delivers highly focused, high-dose radiation to precisely defined target volumes in one or few fractions using stereotactic image guidance. In recurrent or residual chordoma, SRS provides salvage local control by delivering ablative radiation doses to focal tumor deposits while minimizing re-irradiation of previously treated normal tissues. When combined with immunotherapy (nivolumab), SRS may additionally induce immunogenic cell death and abscopal immune responses.

key_evidence.study_name: Stereotactic Radiosurgery for Skull Base Chordoma — Multi-Institutional Experience

treatments.availability: Widely available at major medical centers — CyberKnife, Gamma Knife, LINAC-based SRS

treatments.name: ERAS-601 (SHP2 Inhibitor) + Cetuximab

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 1b/2 FLAGSHP-1 trial with 11 chordoma patients as of October 2023. Early but encouraging data showing tumor shrinkage in 7/8 evaluable combination patients. SHP2 identified as genetic dependency for chordoma.

survival_benefit.rationale: 1 partial response and 8 stable disease in 9 combination patients, with 7/8 showing some tumor shrinkage. Early data but signal of meaningful activity in a refractory population. Represents novel mechanism not previously targeted.

accessibility.rationale: Clinical trial only — Phase 1b/2 at MSK and select sites. ERAS-601 is investigational. Cetuximab is FDA-approved (off-label). Enrollment actively recruiting for chordoma cohort in 2025-2026.

safety_profile.rationale: Most common toxicities dermatologic (related to cetuximab) and reversible. No dose-limiting toxicities reported in chordoma cohort. SHP2 inhibitor class generally well tolerated.

biomarker_match.rationale: SHP2 signaling identified as genetic dependency for chordoma, making virtually all chordomas potential responders. No specific biomarker selection required. EGFR expression enhances cetuximab component activity.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: ERAS-601 is a potent allosteric inhibitor of SHP2 (PTPN11), a phosphatase that functions as a critical signaling node in the RAS/MAPK pathway. SHP2 has been identified as a genetic dependency for chordoma through functional genomic screens. By inhibiting SHP2, ERAS-601 blocks RAS/MAPK signaling essential for chordoma proliferation. Combination with cetuximab (anti-EGFR monoclonal antibody) provides dual pathway suppression, as EGFR signals through SHP2 to activate RAS, creating synthetic lethality when both nodes are targeted.

key_evidence.study_name: FLAGSHP-1: Phase 1 Study of ERAS-601 as Monotherapy or in Combination with Cetuximab in Advanced Chordoma

treatments.availability: Clinical trial only — FLAGSHP-1 Phase 1b/2, actively recruiting at MSK and select US centers

treatments.name: Cetuximab (Anti-EGFR Monoclonal Antibody)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase II trial actively recruiting (MD Anderson). Preliminary results presented at International Chordoma Research Workshop showing patient benefit. Case reports of neurological symptom improvement. Limited published data.

survival_benefit.rationale: Preliminary reports indicate disease stabilization and some tumor shrinkage. Case series data suggest symptom improvement including neurological deficits. Full phase II results pending publication.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved for colorectal cancer and head/neck cancer. IV infusion requiring clinic visits. Phase II trial at MD Anderson actively recruiting. Available off-label but limited chordoma evidence for insurance coverage.

safety_profile.rationale: Well-characterized safety from CRC and H&N cancer use. Acneiform rash, infusion reactions, hypomagnesemia are main toxicities. Generally manageable. Rare severe infusion reactions.

biomarker_match.rationale: EGFR expression present in majority of chordomas. Targets extracellular domain of EGFR receptor. Complements intracellular SHP2 inhibition in combination approaches.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Cetuximab is a chimeric human/mouse IgG1 monoclonal antibody that binds to the extracellular domain of EGFR with high affinity, preventing ligand binding and receptor activation. It also induces EGFR internalization and degradation, and mediates antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity (ADCC). In EGFR-expressing chordoma, cetuximab blocks EGFR-driven proliferative signaling through the RAS/MAPK and PI3K/AKT pathways.

key_evidence.study_name: Phase II Trial of Cetuximab for EGFR-Expressing Advanced or Metastatic Chordoma (MD Anderson)

treatments.availability: FDA-approved for CRC/H&N — Phase II trial at MD Anderson actively recruiting for chordoma

treatments.name: Palbociclib (CDK4/6 Inhibitor)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase II NCT PMO-1601 trial in 28 patients with p16/CDKN2A-loss chordoma. Final results published 2025. Met predetermined threshold (DCR 39% vs 25% target). No objective responses — all stable disease.

survival_benefit.rationale: Disease control rate 39% at 6 cycles. Median PFS 5.6 months. Median OS 24.6 months. No objective responses achieved. Cytostatic effect only. Identifies potential combination approaches via PIK3CA/PTEN mutations.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved for HR+/HER2- breast cancer. Oral medication, widely available. Requires biomarker selection (p16 loss, retained RB1). Off-label use possible.

safety_profile.rationale: Grade 3+ adverse events in 39.2%. Neutropenia most common (28.5%). Requires dose adjustment and monitoring. Manageable with clinical oversight.

biomarker_match.rationale: Requires p16/CDKN2A loss and retained CDK4/6 and RB1 expression. Present in >50% of chordomas but requires molecular testing. PIK3CA mutations associated with early progression — potential exclusion factor.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Palbociclib selectively inhibits cyclin-dependent kinases 4 and 6 (CDK4/6), blocking phosphorylation of retinoblastoma protein (RB) and arresting cells in G1 phase of the cell cycle. In chordoma, loss of the endogenous CDK4/6 inhibitor p16 (CDKN2A) leads to constitutive CDK4/6 activation and unrestrained cell cycle progression. Pharmacologic CDK4/6 inhibition with palbociclib restores cell cycle control in p16-deficient chordoma cells.

key_evidence.study_name: CDK4/6 Inhibition in Advanced Chordoma: Final Results of the NCT PMO-1601 Trial

treatments.availability: FDA-approved for breast cancer — off-label for biomarker-selected chordoma

treatments.name: Erlotinib (EGFR Tyrosine Kinase Inhibitor)

evidence_level.rationale: Case reports and small case series only (5 studies). Two patients showed partial response with significant tumor bulk reduction (70% and 46%). No prospective clinical trial data. Xenograft model data supporting activity.

survival_benefit.rationale: Significant tumor reduction in select patients (70% and 46% in case reports). However, case report level evidence — unclear how generalizable. Preclinical xenograft data shows growth inhibition.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved for NSCLC and pancreatic cancer. Oral medication, widely available, generic formulations reduce cost. Easily prescribed off-label.

safety_profile.rationale: Well-characterized from extensive NSCLC use. Rash, diarrhea main side effects. Generally well tolerated. Lower potency against chordoma than afatinib preclinically.

biomarker_match.rationale: EGFR expression common in chordoma. Erlotinib is a reversible EGFR inhibitor — less potent than afatinib against chordoma cell lines (IC50 0.1-0.8 vs 0.014-0.023 umol/L).

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Erlotinib is a reversible small-molecule inhibitor of EGFR tyrosine kinase. It competitively inhibits ATP binding at the EGFR kinase domain, blocking downstream RAS/MAPK and PI3K/AKT signaling. In chordoma, EGFR overexpression drives proliferation, and erlotinib inhibits this signal. However, as a reversible inhibitor, it is less potent than the irreversible inhibitor afatinib against chordoma cell lines.

key_evidence.study_name: Erlotinib Inhibits Growth of a Patient-Derived Chordoma Xenograft

treatments.availability: FDA-approved — oral, generic available, widely accessible for off-label use

treatments.name: BN-Brachyury Vaccine (MVA-BN-Brachyury-TRICOM) + Radiation

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 2 BRACHY-CHOR-001 trial with 29 patients. 2 partial responses, PFS >2 years in responders. Did not meet predefined efficacy threshold of 4/29 responses but showed proof-of-concept immunogenicity. Targets the pathognomonic brachyury antigen.

survival_benefit.rationale: 2 partial responses out of 29 patients (6.9% ORR). Progression-free survival >2 years in responders. Did not meet primary endpoint. However, several patients with stable disease showed tumor volume reduction below RECIST threshold. Incremental benefit.

accessibility.rationale: Investigational vaccine — available only through clinical trials. FDA Orphan Drug Designation granted to BN-Brachyury. TAEK-VAC-HerBy next-generation trial ongoing. Limited to select research centers.

safety_profile.rationale: Excellent safety profile confirmed across all vaccine trials. Toxicities primarily injection site reactions and mild systemic symptoms. Vaccine-related serious adverse events rare. Well tolerated in combination with radiation.

biomarker_match.rationale: Directly targets brachyury/TBXT, which is pathognomonic for chordoma and universally expressed. Brachyury is virtually absent in normal adult tissues, providing ideal tumor-associated antigen specificity. Perfect biomarker match for TBXT-positive patient.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: BN-Brachyury is a therapeutic cancer vaccine using a heterologous prime-boost strategy: priming with Modified Vaccinia Ankara (MVA-BN)-Brachyury and boosting with Fowlpox Virus (FPV)-Brachyury. Both vectors co-express the brachyury antigen and three human costimulatory molecules (TRICOM: B7-1, ICAM-1, LFA-3). The vaccine induces brachyury-specific CD4+ and CD8+ T cell responses that seek to destroy cells expressing brachyury, which is the pathognomonic transcription factor driving chordoma and an embryonic transcription factor driving epithelial-mesenchymal transition.

key_evidence.study_name: Phase 2 Study of BN-Brachyury Vaccine in Combination with Radiation for Advanced Chordoma (BRACHY-CHOR-001)

treatments.availability: Clinical trial only — TAEK-VAC-HerBy (next-generation) trial ongoing; FDA Orphan Drug Designation

treatments.name: Nivolumab (Anti-PD-1 Checkpoint Inhibitor) +/- Stereotactic Radiosurgery

evidence_level.rationale: NCT03173950 ongoing trial for rare CNS cancers including chordoma. Interim analysis showed 1 patient with stable disease >=6 months. Pediatric case report showed PR correlating with brachyury immune recognition. Small numbers but biological rationale supported by PD-L1 expression.

survival_benefit.rationale: Limited data — 1 patient with stable disease >=6 months in interim analysis. Pediatric case report showed partial response with immune recognition of brachyury. Combination with SRS may enhance abscopal effect. Early-stage investigation.

accessibility.rationale: Nivolumab is FDA-approved for multiple cancers. Off-label use possible but limited chordoma-specific evidence for insurance coverage. Clinical trial enrollment at Johns Hopkins and MSK for combination with SRS.

safety_profile.rationale: Well-characterized immune checkpoint inhibitor safety profile. Risk of immune-related adverse events (colitis, hepatitis, pneumonitis, endocrinopathies). Acceptable safety in rare CNS cancer trial.

biomarker_match.rationale: Chordoma tumors express PD-L1 and contain tumor-infiltrating CD8+ T cells, providing rationale. However, PD-L1 expression varies. Brachyury-specific immune recognition in INI1-deficient cases. Limited predictive biomarker data for response.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Nivolumab is a fully human IgG4 monoclonal antibody that blocks the PD-1 receptor on T cells, preventing engagement with PD-L1/PD-L2 ligands on tumor cells. This releases the immune checkpoint brake, reactivating exhausted tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes. In chordoma, PD-L1 expression on tumor cells and presence of CD8+ TILs provide the prerequisite for checkpoint blockade efficacy. Combination with stereotactic radiosurgery aims to induce immunogenic cell death, releasing tumor neoantigens (including brachyury) to enhance T cell priming and abscopal responses.

key_evidence.study_name: Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor Nivolumab in People with Recurrent Select Rare CNS Cancers (NCT03173950)

treatments.availability: FDA-approved (off-label); clinical trial NCT03173950 at Johns Hopkins and MSK for combination with SRS

treatments.name: GI-6301 (Yeast-Brachyury Vaccine)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase I (8 patients) and Phase II RCT (24 patients) completed. Phase I showed immune response induction and disease stabilization in 5/8 patients. Phase II did not demonstrate benefit over placebo with radiation. Trial stopped early.

survival_benefit.rationale: Phase II: Median PFS 20.6 months (vaccine) vs 25.9 months (placebo) — no benefit demonstrated. One PR in each arm. Phase I: 5/8 had disease stabilization, 1 had >30% shrinkage. Overall, did not meet efficacy endpoints.

accessibility.rationale: Clinical development uncertain after Phase II failure. No active clinical trials with GI-6301. Concept validated that brachyury-targeting vaccines can induce immune responses, but clinical translation remains unproven.

safety_profile.rationale: Well tolerated with minimal vaccine-specific toxicity. Side effects primarily injection site reactions. All toxicities were related to concurrent radiation, not the vaccine.

biomarker_match.rationale: Directly targets brachyury/TBXT — pathognomonic for chordoma and universally expressed. Perfect antigen specificity. Demonstrates that brachyury-specific T cell responses can be induced.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: GI-6301 is a heat-killed recombinant yeast (Saccharomyces cerevisiae) vaccine engineered to express the full-length human brachyury (TBXT) protein. Upon administration, yeast particles are phagocytosed by dendritic cells and macrophages, processed, and presented via MHC class I and II pathways to induce brachyury-specific CD4+ helper and CD8+ cytotoxic T cell responses. The yeast platform provides inherent adjuvant activity through beta-glucan activation of innate immunity.

key_evidence.study_name: Randomized, Double-Blind, Placebo-Controlled Phase II Study of GI-6301 in Combination with Radiotherapy in Locally Advanced Chordoma

treatments.availability: Not currently in active clinical development — concept validated but clinical path uncertain

treatments.name: TAEK-VAC-HerBy (Next-Generation Brachyury Vaccine — IV Administration)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 2 trial ongoing with IV administration. Represents next-generation evolution of BN-Brachyury platform with enhanced immune stimulation. Very early data — trial still recruiting. Based on learnings from three prior brachyury vaccine trials.

survival_benefit.rationale: Preclinical rationale strong — IV administration may improve systemic immune priming. 1 RECIST response and multiple stable disease in early Phase 1. Reduced pain symptoms in 2 patients. Full efficacy data pending.

accessibility.rationale: Investigational — available only through Phase 2 clinical trial. Limited center availability. Bavarian Nordic sponsorship. Recruitment ongoing.

safety_profile.rationale: Phase 1 IV administration showed acceptable safety. Vaccine platform well tolerated across prior trials. No dose-limiting toxicities reported. IV route may increase systemic immune activation.

biomarker_match.rationale: Targets brachyury/TBXT — pathognomonic for chordoma. Universal expression in all chordoma types. Ideal tumor-associated antigen with minimal normal tissue expression.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: TAEK-VAC-HerBy represents a next-generation brachyury-targeting vaccine platform from Bavarian Nordic with intravenous administration for enhanced systemic immune activation. The IV route aims to improve antigen presentation and T cell priming compared to subcutaneous delivery used in prior trials. Enhanced formulation designed to overcome immune evasion mechanisms identified in earlier brachyury vaccine trials.

key_evidence.study_name: Phase 2 Study of TAEK-VAC-HerBy in Advanced Chordoma

treatments.availability: Clinical trial only — Phase 2, actively recruiting

clinical_trials.title: Phase 1b/2 Study of ERAS-601 (SHP2 Inhibitor) as Monotherapy or in Combination with Cetuximab in Advanced Chordoma
clinical_trials.early_results: In 9 patients on combination, 1 PR and 8 SD with 7/8 showing tumor shrinkage. Encouraging preliminary activity with manageable dermatologic toxicity.

clinical_trials.title: Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor Nivolumab in People with Recurrent Select Rare CNS Cancers
biomarker_requirements: Recurrent rare CNS cancer including chordoma
clinical_trials.early_results: Interim analysis: 1 chordoma patient with SD >= 6 months. Acceptable safety profile. Non-heavily pretreated cohort showing signals.

clinical_trials.title: Randomized Phase II Study of GI-6301 (Yeast-Brachyury) Vaccine + RT vs Placebo + RT in Locally Advanced Chordoma
clinical_trials.early_results: 24 patients enrolled. Median PFS 20.6 months (vaccine) vs 25.9 months (placebo). Trial stopped early — no significant benefit demonstrated. Vaccine well tolerated.

clinical_trials.title: Phase II Trial of Cetuximab for Patients with EGFR-Expressing Advanced or Metastatic Chordoma
clinical_trials.early_results: Preliminary results presented at International Chordoma Research Workshop — patients showing clinical benefit. Full results pending.

clinical_trials.title: Phase II Study of Regorafenib in Advanced Chordoma (REGOBONE)
clinical_trials.early_results: 27 patients. PFS 8.2 months (regorafenib) vs 10.1 months (placebo). No objective responses. Did not meet primary endpoint. No signal of benefit.

clinical_trials.title: Phase 2 Study of BN-Brachyury Vaccine + Radiation Therapy in Advanced Chordoma
clinical_trials.early_results: 29 patients. 2 partial responses. PFS >2 years in responders. Did not meet predefined efficacy threshold. Safety confirmed. FDA Orphan Drug Designation granted.

clinical_trials.title: Phase II Study of Palbociclib (CDK4/6 Inhibitor) in Advanced Chordoma with p16/CDKN2A Loss
clinical_trials.early_results: 28 patients. DCR 39% (met threshold). Median PFS 5.6 months, median OS 24.6 months. No objective responses. Grade 3+ neutropenia 28.5%.

combination_strategies.rationale: mTOR pathway activation drives resistance to PDGFR inhibition alone. Dual targeting of PDGFR and mTOR yields 20.9% ORR by Choi criteria (vs 2% with imatinib alone) and 76.7% clinical benefit rate. Addresses the most common resistance mechanism to imatinib monotherapy in chordoma.

EGFR signals through SHP2 to activate RAS/MAPK — dual targeting of the extracellular receptor (cetuximab) and intracellular signaling node (ERAS-601) creates synthetic pathway blockade. 7/8 evaluable combination patients showed tumor shrinkage. Novel mechanism for chordoma-specific dependency.

Radiation induces immunogenic cell death, releasing brachyury antigen to enhance vaccine-induced T cell responses. Radiation also upregulates MHC-I and death receptors on surviving tumor cells, making them more susceptible to immune attack. Combines direct cytotoxic and immune-mediated mechanisms.

SRS induces immunogenic cell death and releases tumor neoantigens including brachyury. Nivolumab removes PD-1/PD-L1 checkpoint brake to allow reinvigorated T cell responses against released antigens. Chordoma expresses PD-L1 and harbors CD8+ TILs — prerequisites for checkpoint blockade synergy with radiation.

Afatinib degrades brachyury protein, potentially radiosensitizing chordoma cells by removing a key survival factor. EGFR inhibition also impairs DNA damage repair. Sequential or concurrent use after maximal safe resection could enhance proton therapy efficacy. No prospective trial data yet.

supportive_care.evidence: Chordoma Foundation guidelines; institutional rehabilitation outcomes data showing functional improvement in multiple domains
supportive_care.benefit: Skull base chordoma and its treatment commonly cause cranial nerve deficits (III-XII), including diplopia, facial weakness, swallowing dysfunction, and hearing loss. Specialized neuro-rehabilitation with speech therapy, physical therapy, and occupational therapy demonstrably improves functional outcomes. Prehabilitation before surgery reduces complications and hospital stay length.

supportive_care.approach: Endocrine Replacement Therapy for Radiation-Induced Hypopituitarism
supportive_care.evidence: 17% incidence of hypopituitarism after proton therapy to clivus; standard endocrinology management guidelines
supportive_care.benefit: Proton/carbon ion therapy to the clivus region invariably exposes the pituitary gland and hypothalamus to radiation. Hypopituitarism occurs in ~17% of patients, requiring lifelong hormone replacement (cortisol, thyroid, testosterone, growth hormone). Regular endocrine monitoring essential for quality of life and preventing adrenal crisis.

supportive_care.evidence: Chordoma Foundation survivorship guidelines; systematic assessment showing somatic and neuropathic pain components
supportive_care.benefit: Chordoma patients experience both somatic pain (bone/tissue destruction) and neuropathic pain (nerve compression/invasion). Multimodal approach using analgesics, neuropathic agents (gabapentin/pregabalin), interventional pain techniques, and psychological support. Skull base location with brainstem extension causes headache, facial pain, and occipital neuralgia requiring specialized management.

supportive_care.evidence: Quality of life studies showing depression as significant determinant of QoL in chordoma patients post-treatment; Chordoma Foundation survivorship program
supportive_care.benefit: Skull base chordoma patients have significantly lower quality of life than the general population. Depression, anxiety, and adjustment disorder are common due to rare disease isolation, facial/cranial nerve disfigurement, prolonged treatment course, and recurrence fear. Chordoma Foundation peer support, individual therapy, and online communities provide evidence-based psychosocial benefit.

supportive_care.evidence: Clivus chordoma with brainstem extension commonly affects lower cranial nerves (IX, X, XII) controlling swallowing
supportive_care.benefit: Dysphagia risk from tumor compression or surgical/radiation injury to lower cranial nerves. Speech-language pathology assessment, modified diet textures, swallowing exercises, and nutritional supplementation prevent aspiration pneumonia and malnutrition. Critical for maintaining performance status during systemic therapy.

supportive_care.evidence: Vision loss <3% grade 3+ after proton therapy; cranial nerve III/IV/VI palsy risk from tumor and surgery
supportive_care.benefit: Clivus chordoma proximity to cavernous sinus and cranial nerves III, IV, VI causes diplopia, ptosis, and visual field deficits. Regular ophthalmologic assessment during and after treatment. Prism glasses, patching, or strabismus surgery for persistent diplopia. Monitoring for radiation-induced optic neuropathy after particle therapy.
